Specifying the effect on the wrong scale is a silent error

Interpreting power curves:

Are estimates conditional on their inputs
Should be accompanied by sensitivity analyses
Do not guarantee adequate power — they estimate it under stated assumptions

title: 17  Simulation-Based Power Analysis
text:
17.10 Further reading






TipUseful resources





Paper: Gelman, A. & Carlin, J. (2014). Beyond power calculations: Assessing Type S (sign) and Type M (magnitude) errors. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 9(6), 641–651.

Paper: Button, K.S. et al. (2013). Power failure: why small sample size undermines the reliability of neuroscience. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 14(5), 365–376.

Book: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences (2nd ed.). Lawrence Erlbaum Associates.

Package: The pwr package provides formula-based power calculations for simple designs as a complement to simulation.

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.1 Introduction
This workshop introduces AI-assisted programming tools to help you write better R code and create publication-ready visualisations. You’ll learn to use GitHub Copilot for code suggestions and GitHub Copilot Chat for planning and problem-solving.
Workshop focus: Creating code you understand and can verify, with emphasis on debugging and high-quality data visualisation.

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.1.1 What Are Large Language Models?
GitHub Copilot uses Large Language Models (LLMs) - AI systems trained on vast amounts of code to predict and generate programming solutions.
What they can do: - Suggest code completions as you type - Generate code from natural language descriptions - Help identify and fix errors - Answer questions about R functions
Critical limitations: - May generate incorrect or outdated code - Cannot understand your specific data or research context - Suggestions vary in quality - No guarantee of correctness






WarningYou Are Responsible



Always verify AI suggestions. Test code with your data, ensure you understand what it does, and confirm it produces correct results. AI accelerates learning - it doesn’t replace understanding.



18.1.2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, you will:

Use GitHub Copilot for code completion in RStudio
Write effective prompts for high-quality suggestions
Use AI to debug and understand errors in your code
Use GitHub Chat for project organisation
Create publication-ready visualisations following best practices
Critically evaluate and verify all AI suggestions

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.2 Part 1: Getting Started

18.2.1 Access GitHub Copilot
Prerequisites: 1. Verify student status at https://education.github.com/ 2. Activate free GitHub Copilot access 3. Have RStudio installed

18.2.2 Enable Copilot in RStudio
Steps:

Open RStudio
Navigate to Tools > Global Options > Copilot

Check “Enable GitHub Copilot”

Click “Sign in to GitHub” and authenticate

Verify it works:
Create a new R script and type:

# Calculate the mean of

You should see grey “ghost text” suggesting a completion.
Key controls: - Tab = Accept suggestion - Esc = Reject suggestion - Keep typing = Get new suggestion

18.2.3 Controlling Copilot Behaviour
Sometimes you want to work without AI assistance to test your understanding.
To temporarily disable Copilot:
Method 1: Command Palette 1. Press Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Shift+P (Mac) 2. Type “Copilot: Disable Completions” 3. Press Enter
Method 2: Status Bar 1. Look for the Copilot icon in the bottom-right of RStudio 2. Click it to see options 3. Toggle “Completions” off
To re-enable: - Use the same methods and select “Enable Completions”






TipPractise Without AI



After completing exercises with Copilot: 1. Turn it off 2. Try recreating the code yourself 3. This tests whether you understood the suggestions or just accepted them
This is crucial for learning - AI should accelerate understanding, not replace it.








NoteTroubleshooting



If suggestions don’t appear: - Check you’re signed into GitHub in RStudio - Verify internet connection - Restart RStudio if needed - Ensure Copilot is enabled in Global Options

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.3 Part 2: How Context Influences Suggestions

18.3.1 The Context Principle
Copilot learns from the current file only. To demonstrate this, we’ll work with the palmerpenguins dataset throughout this workshop - a biological dataset perfect for understanding ecological patterns.

18.3.2 Understanding What Influences Suggestions
High impact on Copilot suggestions: 1. ✅ Comments immediately before your code 2. ✅ Code patterns earlier in the same file 3. ✅ Variable names already defined 4. ✅ Library calls at the top of your script
Low/No impact: - ❌ Files open in other tabs - ❌ Other files in your project - ❌ Separate style guide files - ❌ Previous conversations with Chat

18.3.3 Exercise 1: Context Matters - Before and After
We’ll create the same code twice - once with no context, once with context - to see the difference.






Note



These exercises are designed to work with Github copilot, but can be adapted for use with Microsoft copilot or other chat tools if needed, just type these comments into an LLM chat instead



18.3.3.1 Version A: No Context (Poor Suggestions)
Task: Open a NEW blank script (don’t include any setup code).
Type only this:

# Calculate mean body mass by species

Pause and observe what Copilot suggests:
What you might get:

mean(body_mass)  # Generic, doesn't know data frame or variable names
# or
mean(x$body_mass)  # Assumes unknown variable names

Problems with these suggestions: - Doesn’t know the data frame is called penguins - Doesn’t know the variable is body_mass_g (with units) - Doesn’t group by species - Might not even run without errors

18.3.3.2 Version B: With Context (Better Suggestions)
Now start a NEW script with context at the top:

# Penguin body mass analysis
# Data: palmerpenguins package
# Method: tidyverse with native pipe |>

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
data("penguins")

# Example: tidyverse style analysis
example <- penguins |>
  filter(!is.na(flipper_length_mm)) |>
  select(species, body_mass_g)

# Now calculate mean body mass by species

Pause after typing the comment and observe what Copilot suggests.
Expected better suggestion:

penguins |>
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))

This is better because: - Uses correct data frame name (penguins) - Uses correct variable name (body_mass_g) - Includes grouping by species - Handles missing values (na.rm = TRUE) - Uses tidyverse pipe style






NoteClass Activity: Compare Results



Turn to a partner and discuss:

What did Copilot suggest in Version A?
What did it suggest in Version B?
Were your suggestions identical to your partner’s?
Did you need to modify the suggestion to get the answer you needed?

Share with class: What was most surprising about the difference?



18.3.4 Exercise 2: Your Turn - Build Context


Calculate the median bill length and bill depth for male and female penguins of each species.



Turn Copilot OFF (use Ctrl+Shift+P, “Copilot: Disable”)
On paper, write:

What variables you need
What grouping you need
What summary statistics to calculate


Turn Copilot back ON
Write a detailed comment with all this context
Observe what Copilot suggests







TipExample Context Comment






# Data: penguins dataset
# Variables: species, sex, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm
# Remove rows with missing sex values
# Group by species AND sex
# Calculate median bill length and median bill depth
# Include sample size for each group
# Use tidyverse with native pipe |>

Expected suggestion:

penguins |>
  filter(!is.na(sex)) |>
  group_by(species, sex) |>
  summarise(
    median_bill_length = median(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_bill_depth = median(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )







18.3.5 What Actually Influences Inline Suggestions - Summary
What helps most:


Detailed comment immediately before code - this matters most

Be specific about data frame names
List exact variable names
Specify method (tidyverse, base R, etc.)
State what output should look like



Code patterns earlier in same file - helps moderately

Example code using your style
Library calls showing which packages you use
Variable names already defined



Things that DON’T help:

Other files in your project
Files open in other tabs
Previous sessions or scripts









ImportantKey Takeaway



To get good suggestions from Copilot:
Write detailed, specific comments immediately before your code.
That’s it. That’s the secret.
Everything else has minimal impact.

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
section: 18.4 Part 3: Using AI for Debugging (25 minutes)
text:
18.4 Part 3: Using AI for Debugging (25 minutes)
AI is most useful for debugging - finding and fixing errors in code YOU wrote. This is where AI truly shines because:

You wrote the code (you understand the intent)
AI spots syntax errors you missed
Explaining errors helps you understand them
You maintain ownership of the logic and learning


18.4.1 Why Debugging with AI is Better Than Using AI to Write Code
When AI writes code: - ⚠️ You might not understand it - ⚠️ You can’t verify if it’s correct - ⚠️ You don’t learn the logic - ⚠️ You can’t modify it later
When AI helps you debug: - ✅ You wrote the original code (you know what you intended) - ✅ AI helps you understand YOUR mistakes - ✅ You learn from errors - ✅ You can apply fixes to future code






ImportantDebugging First, Writing Second



In this workshop, we emphasise debugging over code generation because:


You learn more from fixing mistakes than accepting suggestions

You maintain control over logic and approach

You build skills that transfer to new problems

You understand what went wrong and why

Use AI to help you fix YOUR code, not to write code you don’t understand.



18.4.2 Setup: Load Data for Debugging Examples
Make a new script and make sure you have this at the top of your script:

# Load packages and data for debugging exercises
library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
data("penguins")


18.4.3 Error Type 1: Simple Syntax Errors
Scenario: You want to create a bar plot showing the count of penguins for each species.
Your broken code:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species)) +
  geom_barchart()

Error message:
Error in geom_barchart(): could not find function "geom_barchart"

18.4.3.1 Exercise: Debug with Copilot
Instructions:

Copy BOTH the code AND the error message
Paste them as a comment in your script:


# This code gives an error:
# ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species)) +
#   geom_barchart()
# 
# Error: could not find function "geom_barchart"
#
# Fix this code:


Let Copilot suggest a fix after the “Fix this code:” line

Don’t just accept it - read the suggestion first







TipExpected Fix






ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species)) +
  geom_bar()  # Correct function name is geom_bar()

What you should learn:


Why the error occurred: The function is geom_bar() not geom_barchart()


How to verify: Check ggplot2 documentation or type ??geom_bar


How to find this yourself: Read the error carefully - “could not find function” means typo or wrong package

Check your understanding: - What happens if you type ??geom_barchart? (Help says “No documentation”) - What happens if you type ??geom_bar? (Help shows correct documentation) - How would you have found this without AI? (Read the error, check spelling, search “ggplot bar plot”)




18.4.4 Error Type 2: Logic Errors (More Complex)
These are harder because there’s no error message - the code runs but gives wrong results.
Scenario: You want the mean body mass for Biscoe island penguins.
Your code (runs but gives wrong answer):

biscoe_penguins <- penguins |>
  filter(island == "biscoe")  # PROBLEM: wrong capitalisation

biscoe_penguins |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g))

Output:

# A tibble: 1 × 1
  mean_mass
      <dbl>
1       NaN


18.4.4.1 Understanding the Problem
This is tricky because: - ❌ No error message (code runs successfully) - ❌ Result looks plausible (NaN could mean missing data) - ✅ Need to understand your data to spot the issue - ✅ Need to know what reasonable values should be

18.4.4.2 Exercise: Debug with Context
Step 1: Understand your data
Before asking AI for help, check the data structure:

# Check how island is coded in the data
summary(penguins$island)

What this tells you: - There ARE Biscoe penguins - Island names are capitalised - Your filter should have found them
Step 2: Count how many penguins your filter found

biscoe_penguins <- penguins |>
  filter(island == "biscoe")

# How many did we get?
nrow(biscoe_penguins)

Result: 0 rows! That’s the problem.
Step 3: Now ask AI with full context
Copy this into a comment or paste into GitHub Chat:

# I'm trying to calculate mean body mass for Biscoe island penguins.

# My code:

#biscoe_penguins <- penguins |>
#  filter(island == "biscoe")

#biscoe_penguins |>
#  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g))


#Result: NaN

#Data structure shows:

# summary(penguins$island)
# Biscoe (168), Dream (124), Torgersen (52)

# But my filtered data has 0 rows. Why?







TipExpected AI Response





AI should identify:


The problem: island == "biscoe" uses lowercase

The data: Island values are “Biscoe” (capitalised)

The effect: Filter returns 0 rows

The result: mean() of empty vector gives NaN

Correct code:

biscoe_penguins <- penguins |>
  filter(island == "Biscoe")  # Correct capitalisation

biscoe_penguins |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE))





18.4.5 Error Type 3: Variable Mapping in ggplot2
Scenario: You want to show bill length vs bill depth, coloured by species.
Your code:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point(color = species) +
  labs(title = "Bill Dimensions by Species")

Error:
Error in layer(data = data, mapping = mapping, stat = stat, geom = GeomPoint,  : 
  object 'species' not found

18.4.5.1 Exercise: Debug This
Instructions:

Copy code and error
Paste as a comment in your script
Type “Fix:” and let Copilot suggest

Before accepting: Can you figure out what’s wrong?

Hint: Look at the coursebook chapter on ggplot2 aesthetics.






TipSolution





The error: species is a variable in the data, so it must be inside aes().
Why: Variables that map to visual properties MUST be in aes(). Fixed values (like color = "red") go outside aes().
Correct code:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species)) +  # species inside aes()
  labs(title = "Bill Dimensions by Species")

Reference Coursebook: > “If specifying a fixed aesthetic e.g. red for everything it DOES NOT go inside aes()… If you wish to modify an aesthetic according to a variable in your data THEN it DOES go inside aes()”
Check understanding: - When would you use color = "red"? (When all points should be red) - When would you use aes(color = species)? (When colour represents data)




18.4.6 Debugging Checklist
When code doesn’t work, follow these steps:
Before asking AI:

✅ Read the error message carefully

What function failed?
What does the error say?


✅ Check your data structure


glimpse(penguins) - see variables and types

summary(penguins) - see value ranges

names(penguins) - check exact spelling


✅ Verify variable names

Check capitalisation
Check spelling
Check units (is it body_mass or body_mass_g?)


✅ Test with small data

Try penguins |> slice(1:5) to test on just 5 rows
Easier to spot problems in small output



When asking AI:

✅ Provide complete error message (copy-paste exactly)
✅ Include your code (the exact code that failed)
✅ Explain what you intended (“I wanted to calculate…”)
✅ Show relevant data structure (str(), summary(), glimpse())

After AI suggests a fix:


✅ Understand WHY the error occurred

What was wrong?
Why did it cause this specific error?



✅ Understand WHY the fix works

What did the fix change?
How does it solve the problem?



✅ Test the corrected code

Does it run without errors?
Does it give expected results?
Do the values make biological sense?



✅ Add a comment explaining the fix

# Fixed: Changed "biscoe" to "Biscoe" - island names are capitalised in data
biscoe_penguins <- penguins |>
  filter(island == "Biscoe")









NoteClass Discussion: Debugging Experiences



Turn to a partner and discuss:

Which error type was hardest to debug?
When was AI most helpful vs least helpful?
When did understanding the data help you spot errors AI couldn’t see?
How does debugging with AI compare to debugging alone?
What’s one debugging skill you learned that you’ll use in future?

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
section: 18.5 Part 4: GitHub Chat for Project Organisation
text:
18.5 Part 4: GitHub Chat for Project Organisation

18.5.1 What Is GitHub Chat?
GitHub Chat is a conversational AI tool accessed through your web browser. It helps you plan projects, understand errors, and make coding decisions.
Key difference from inline Copilot: - Inline Copilot: Completes code as you type (sees only current file) - GitHub Chat: Answers questions and discusses problems (you provide context)






WarningImportant: Chat Has No Memory



Chat does NOT automatically know: - ❌ What files are in your repository - ❌ What code you’ve written - ❌ Previous conversations - ❌ Your data or research context
You must provide ALL context in EACH question.



18.5.2 Accessing GitHub Chat
Simple method: 1. Navigate to your repository on GitHub.com 2. Look for the Copilot icon (top-right) 3. Click to open Chat

18.5.3 How to Share Code with Chat
There are two simple ways to give Chat the information it needs:

18.5.3.1 Method 1: Copy-Paste (Simplest)
Use for: Quick debugging, specific errors
How:
I'm getting an error:

penguins |>
  filter(island == "biscoe") |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g))

Error: Result is NaN

The data has 168 Biscoe penguins but my filter returns 0 rows. Why?
Best for beginners - works immediately, no setup needed.

18.5.3.2 Method 2: Reference Repository Files
Use for: Planning projects, reviewing entire files
Requirement: Code must be committed and pushed to GitHub first
How:
Look at my file scripts/01_data_cleaning.R

Should I split the quality checks into a separate section?
Or use the # shortcut - type # and select from your files:
Review my analysis structure:
#scripts/01_data_cleaning.R
#scripts/02_analysis.R

Is this logical?






TipBeginner Workflow





While coding: Copy-paste code snippets for quick help

After committing: Reference files for structure review

For planning: Ask about organisation before writing code

Don’t feel pressured to commit just to ask Chat - copy-paste works fine!

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.6 Using AI Style Guides

18.6.1 Why Create a Style Guide for AI?
When working with AI coding assistants like GitHub Copilot, you’ll ask dozens of questions throughout a project. Without guidance, the AI might suggest code using different styles each time—sometimes base R, sometimes tidyverse; sometimes %>%, sometimes |>; sometimes camelCase, sometimes snake_case. This inconsistency makes your code harder to read, harder to debug, and harder to understand.
A coding style guide solves this problem by giving the AI a consistent set of instructions about how you want it to behave. Think of it as setting expectations at the start of a collaboration: “I always use tidyverse with the native pipe |>, I name variables in snake_case, and I always use colourblind-safe colour palettes.” The AI then tailors its suggestions to match your preferences, creating more consistent, readable code throughout your project.

18.6.2 How GitHub Copilot Uses Style Guides
GitHub Copilot Chat looks for a special file called copilot-instructions.md in the .github/ folder of your repository:
your-project/
├── .github/
│   └── copilot-instructions.md    # AI reads this automatically
├── data/
├── scripts/
└── figures/
When you ask Copilot Chat a question, it automatically reads this file to understand your coding standards (but you can # include it in specific chats to be sure). You don’t need to repeat yourself—the AI already knows your preferences.
Important distinction:

GitHub Copilot Chat (browser-based conversational tool): Reads copilot-instructions.md automatically
Inline Copilot (code completion in RStudio): Only sees the current file you’re editing

This means you still need detailed comments in your R scripts for inline suggestions, but Chat will follow your style guide for planning, debugging, and code review.

18.6.3 What Goes in a Style Guide?
An effective style guide tells the AI about:
1. Project Context
This project analyses Palmer penguin morphological data to understand species differences and ecological patterns.
2. Required Packages and Syntax
- ALWAYS use tidyverse with native pipe |>
- ALWAYS use <- for assignment (not =)
- Load ALL packages at top of script with library()
3. Naming Conventions
- Variables: snake_case (e.g., body_mass_g, mean_flipper_length)
- Data frames: Add _df suffix (e.g., penguin_summary_df)
- Plots: Add _plot suffix (e.g., mass_comparison_plot)
4. Code Organisation
- Maximum 10-15 lines per code chunk
- Comment every chunk explaining WHY, not just WHAT
- Use section headers with ---- to organise scripts
5. Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Not checking variable capitalisation (island == "biscoe" vs "Biscoe")
- Using fill instead of colour for points
- Forgetting na.rm = TRUE in summary functions

18.6.4 Benefits Beyond AI
Creating a style guide isn’t just for AI—it benefits you:

Consistency: Your own code follows the same patterns
Documentation: New collaborators understand your approach
Quality control: Checklist ensures you don’t skip important steps
Learning: Codifies best practices you want to remember

The act of writing down your standards helps you think critically about what makes code readable, maintainable, and reproducible.

18.6.5 Context Injection: The Broader Pattern
The .github/copilot-instructions.md file is an example of context injection or system prompts—a pattern used across many AI tools. The idea is simple: instead of providing the same instructions repeatedly, you give the AI a persistent set of guidelines that shape all future responses.
Other examples of context injection: - ChatGPT “Custom Instructions” (set preferences once, apply to all chats)

Claude “Projects” (provide project context and style guides)
AI coding tools reading .cursorrules or .aicoderc files

The key principle: AI tools can only work with the context you provide. A style guide is a way to provide that context once, efficiently, rather than repeating yourself in every interaction.






TipStart Simple, Grow Over Time



Don’t try to write a perfect style guide on day one. Start with the basics:

Which packages do you use?
Which pipe operator?
What naming convention?

As you encounter issues (capitalisation errors, colour accessibility problems, etc.), add them to your “Common Mistakes” section. Your style guide becomes a living document that captures lessons learned throughout your project.








ImportantRemember: You’re Still Responsible



A style guide helps AI give better suggestions, but you must still verify everything:

Does the code run without errors?
Does it produce correct results?
Does it follow your standards?
Do you understand what it does?

The AI follows your guide, but doesn’t understand your data, your research context, or what “correct” means for your specific analysis. Always test, always verify, always understand.



18.6.6 Exercise: Build Your Coding Standards
Instead of receiving a complete template, let’s identify what standards YOUR project needs.

18.6.6.1 Step 1: Create the File
In your repository, create:
.github/copilot-instructions.md
Why .github/ folder? This is where GitHub Chat looks for project standards.

18.6.6.2 Step 2: What Standards Do You Need? (Pair Work)
Discuss with a partner and write down:

Packages: Which will you always use? Where should library() calls go?

Syntax:

Native pipe |> or magrittr %>%?
Assignment <- or =?
Variable naming: snake_case, camelCase, or other?



Code Organisation:

How long should code chunks be?
What should comments explain?



Mistakes to Avoid:

What errors did you encounter in Part 3?
What feedback have you had on your code in the past?
How could standards prevent them?



18.6.6.3 Step 3: Build Your Template
Start with this structure and fill in YOUR decisions and additions:






Note





# R Coding Standards for [Your Project]

## Project Context
[2-3 sentences: What are you analysing and why?]

## Package Management
**Required packages:**
- tidyverse
- palmerpenguins  
- viridis

**Rules:**
- Load ALL packages at top of script
- Use `library()` not `require()`

## Syntax Preferences
**Pipe:** Always use `|>` (not `%>%`)
**Assignment:** Always use `<-` (not `=`)
**Naming:** snake_case for all variables

## Code Organisation
**Chunks:** 10-15 lines maximum
**Comments:** Explain WHY, not just WHAT
**Headers:** Use `----` to create sections

## File Organization

### Directory Structureproject/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/           # Original data - NEVER modify
│   └── processed/     # Cleaned data
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_data_cleaning.R
│   ├── 02_exploratory_analysis.R
│   ├── 03_statistical_tests.R
│   └── 04_create_figures.R
├── figures/
│   ├── exploratory/   # Quick plots for checking
│   └── publication/   # Final figures for paper
├── output/
│   └── results/       # Statistical test outputs
└── README.md

## Visualisation Standards
**Required:**
- Colourblind-safe palettes (viridis)
- Axis labels with units
- Sample sizes where relevant
- Professional theme (theme_minimal)

## Common Mistakes to Avoid
[Add mistakes from Part 3:]
- Not checking capitalisation (island == "biscoe" vs "Biscoe")
- Using fill instead of colour for points
- Forgetting na.rm = TRUE
- [Add others you encountered]

## Getting Help from Chat
**Always provide:**
1. Your code (exact copy-paste)
2. Error message (complete text)
3. What you expected vs what happened
4. Data structure (glimpse() output)




18.6.7 Using Chat for Project Planning
Now use Chat to validate and extend your thinking.
Commit your .github/copilot-instructions.md file, then ask:
I'm analysing Palmer penguin data to compare body size between species.

My coding standards are in:
#.github/copilot-instructions.md

Questions:
1. What R scripts should I create and in what order?
2. What should each script do?
3. What should my first analysis steps be?
Chat will review your standards and suggest a project structure.






NoteClass Discussion



Compare Chat’s responses:

What structure did Chat suggest?
Are suggestions similar across groups?
How did your standards file help Chat?
What would you modify?

Share one insight from Chat that surprised you.



18.6.8 When to Use Chat vs Inline Copilot


Task
Inline Copilot
Chat



Writing code
✅ Fast
❌ Too slow


Simple syntax fix
✅ Instant
❌ Overkill


Planning project
❌ Can’t help
✅ Excellent


Understanding errors
⚠️ Brief hint
✅ Full explanation


Choosing approaches
❌ Just suggests
✅ Explains trade-offs


Quick debugging
✅ One-line fix
❌ Too much



Simple rule: Chat for thinking and planning, Copilot for typing and fixing.

18.6.9 Tips for Effective Chat Questions
✅ Good question structure:
Context: I'm analysing penguin body mass by species

My code:
[paste code]

Error/Issue: [what's wrong]

Question: [specific question]
❌ Avoid vague questions:
"My code doesn't work"
"Help with ggplot"
"Make this better"
✅ Be specific:
"Why does my filter return 0 rows when the data has 168 Biscoe penguins?"
"Should I use faceted or grouped boxplots for 3 species × 3 islands?"






TipQuick Reference: Good Chat Habits




Start fresh if Chat seems confused (click “New chat”)
Copy-paste exactly - don’t retype code or errors
Provide context every time - Chat doesn’t remember
Ask for explanation not just solutions
Verify everything Chat suggests - it can be wrong




18.6.10 Brief Note: Chat vs Agents
You may hear about “AI agents” - autonomous systems that can execute multiple steps and make decisions independently.
For this workshop, we’re using Chat because: - ✅ You control every decision - ✅ You learn by engaging with each step
- ✅ Better for understanding and skill-building
Agents are powerful but can hide the learning process. Master Chat first, explore agents later.

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.7 Part 5: Publication-Ready Visualisations
This section focuses on creating high-quality plots following data visualisation best practices from your Coursebook.
Key principle: AI can generate code quickly, but YOU must ensure it follows best practices for publication-quality figures.

18.7.1 Visualisation Best Practices Review
Before creating plots with AI, review these principles from your Coursebook:
From the ggplot2 chapter:

Data must map clearly to visual properties
Choose appropriate geoms for your data type
Layer elements thoughtfully
Use clear, informative labels
Use appropriate axis limits and breaks
Transform scales when needed for clarity
Order categories logically
Use colourblind-friendly palettes
Ensure sufficient contrast
Test how plot looks in greyscale

18.7.2 Exercise 1: Evaluate AI Colour Choices
Scenario: Compare body mass across penguin species.
Basic prompt:

# Create boxplot of body mass by species
# Colour by species

What AI might generate:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass by Species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  )


18.7.2.1 Critical Evaluation
Ask yourself (reference Coursebook colour chapter):


Are these colours colourblind-friendly?

Default ggplot2 colours are NOT colourblind-safe
Red-green combinations are particularly problematic



Is the legend necessary?

X-axis already shows species names
Legend is redundant



Can you see individual data points?

Boxplot shows summary only
Hides the distribution and outliers



Test your understanding: - How would you check if colours are colourblind-safe? - What palette should you use instead? - How could you show both summary AND raw data?






TipImproved Version - Following Best Practices





Better prompt referencing Coursebook standards:

# Create publication-ready boxplot following Coursebook standards
# Body mass by species
# Use colourblind-friendly viridis palette
# Add semi-transparent jittered points to show raw data
# Remove redundant legend
# Include sample sizes in x-axis labels
# Use theme_minimal with readable text

Why this is better: - ✅ Colourblind-safe viridis palette - ✅ Shows both summary (boxplot) AND raw data (points) - ✅ Transparency prevents overplotting - ✅ Sample sizes provide context - ✅ No redundant legend - ✅ Professional, clean theme - ✅ Title explains what is shown









NoteClass Activity: Colour Accessibility Check



Working in pairs:

Generate the basic plot (first version)
Generate the improved plot (second version)
Take a photo of each on your phone
Apply greyscale filter (phone settings or photo app)
Compare: Can you still distinguish species in both versions?









Note






The R package colorBlindness has a function colorBlindness::cvdPlot() That will do this for you.




Discuss: - Which version works better in greyscale? - Why does the viridis palette work better? - What other improvements make the second version clearer?

18.7.3 Exercise 2: Raw Data + Model Summary
Scenario: Examine the relationship between flipper length and body mass.
Poor prompt:

# plot flipper vs mass

What this generates:

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point()

Problems: - Just points, no relationship shown - No indication of species differences - Default colours - Missing labels
Better prompt:

# Create publication-ready scatter plot following Coursebook
# Show relationship between flipper length and body mass
# Include BOTH raw data AND linear model (layered geoms)
# Colour by species with viridis palette
# Use semi-transparent points to show density (alpha)
# Add regression lines per species with confidence intervals
# Include informative labels with units
# Use theme_minimal


18.7.4 Exercise 3: Your Turn - Complete Publication Figure
Create a complete publication-ready figure using ALL Coursebook best practices.
Scenario: Show how bill dimensions (length vs depth) differ between species.
Your task:


Plan first (on paper):

What plot type?
What colours?
Raw data + model?
What labels needed?


Write detailed prompt including all requirements
Generate plot
Evaluate against checklist (below)
Iterate to fix any issues


18.7.4.1 Publication Figure Checklist
Use this to evaluate your figure:
Essential elements:

Raw data visible (with transparency if needed)
Summary/model shown
Colourblind-friendly palette (viridis)
Axis labels with units
Informative title
Professional theme
Sample sizes reported
Saved at 300 DPI

18.7.5 Key Visualisation Principles
When evaluating ANY plot (AI-generated or your own):
Accessibility: Colourblind-friendly? Works in greyscale? Readable text?
Information: Shows raw data? Shows summary? Shows uncertainty?
Clarity: Informative labels? Appropriate scales? Professional theme?






ImportantAI Doesn’t Know Best Practices



AI will generate code that runs, but it won’t automatically follow visualisation best practices unless you specify them.
You must know and specify: - Colourblind-safe palettes (it defaults to rainbow or ggplot2 defaults) - When to show raw data vs summaries (it might show only one) - Clear labelling requirements (it uses variable names as labels) - Sample size reporting (it won’t include unless asked)
Your expertise + AI’s speed = Publication-quality figures
Always evaluate critically and iterate based on Coursebook principles.

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
section: 18.8 Part 6: Best Practices and Responsible Use
text:
18.8 Part 6: Best Practices and Responsible Use

18.8.1 Using AI Responsibly in Research
DO: - ✅ Use AI to help debug YOUR code - ✅ Verify ALL suggestions before accepting - ✅ Test code thoroughly with your data - ✅ Learn from AI suggestions (ask yourself “why?”) - ✅ Reference Coursebook for best practices
DON’T: - ❌ Accept code you don’t understand - ❌ Skip testing and verification - ❌ Let AI make scientific decisions - ❌ Use AI when explicitly prohibited - ❌ Claim AI work as entirely your own

18.8.2 Academic Integrity Checklist
Before submitting any work involving AI assistance:
Understanding: - [ ] I can explain every line of code - [ ] I can modify the code if requirements change - [ ] I understand why this approach was chosen - [ ] I could write similar code independently
Verification: - [ ] Code runs without errors - [ ] Results are correct and make sense - [ ] Visualisations follow best practices - [ ] Statistical methods are appropriate
Attribution: - [ ] AI assistance is disclosed where required - [ ] My original contributions are clear - [ ] Sources (including Coursebook) are cited
Scientific Integrity: - [ ] Data interpretation is my own - [ ] Statistical decisions are justified - [ ] Results are reported honestly

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.9 Summary and Practice Assignment

18.9.1 What We Learned Today
Key Skills:


Context influences AI suggestions - detailed comments matter most

Debugging is the best use of AI - maintains your ownership of learning

Project organisation matters - standards guide both you and AI

Visualisation requires critical evaluation - you ensure quality, not AI

Verification is essential - always test and understand

title: 18  AI-Assisted R Programming
text:
18.10 Additional Resources

18.10.1 R Resources
Data visualisation: - ggplot2 book: https://ggplot2-book.org/ - Data visualisation principles: https://clauswilke.com/dataviz/
General R: - R for Data Science: https://r4ds.had.co.nz/ - RStudio Cheatsheets: https://posit.co/resources/cheatsheets/
AI tools: - GitHub Copilot docs: https://docs.github.com/copilot






NoteRemember the Core Principle



AI is a tool to accelerate learning, not replace it.
The goal is to: - Understand your code - Make informed decisions - Follow best practices - Produce quality research
Use AI strategically, verify everything, and maintain scientific rigour.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
19.1 Introduction
You have used linear regression before to ask whether two variables are related. In this workshop we use it differently. The question is no longer “is methylation associated with age?” — we already have good biological evidence that it is. The question is: given a bat’s methylation profile, how accurately can we predict its age?
This is a machine learning framing. We are not interested in p-values or confidence intervals here. We are interested in prediction accuracy on data the model has never seen. That distinction — inference versus prediction — is the conceptual foundation of everything that follows.
The dataset comes from Wilkinson et al. (2021, Nature Communications), who measured DNA methylation at CpG sites across bat species to build an epigenetic clock: a model that predicts chronological age from methylation patterns. Each row is one bat. The column age is the known age in years. All remaining columns are methylation values at individual CpG sites, expressed as proportions between 0 and 100.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
19.1.1 A note on how we use AI in this workshop
This workshop is divided into two parts. The first is data exploration, where the checks we need to run are well-defined and their outputs are immediately verifiable — you will know whether a result is correct without any modelling knowledge. This is where AI assistance is appropriate, and we use it deliberately.
The second part covers the machine learning methods themselves. Here you will write and run every line of code yourself. If you do not write the modelling code, you will not develop the understanding needed to judge when an AI-generated model is correct, incomplete, or quietly wrong. The ability to critically evaluate AI output depends on having built the underlying knowledge yourself first.

19.1.2 Learning Objectives
By the end of this workshop, you will:

Use GitHub Copilot Chat in agent mode to conduct a structured data exploration
Write precise, verifiable AI prompts and critically evaluate the output
Reframe linear regression as a predictive rather than inferential tool
Construct and justify a train/test split
Fit and evaluate a linear regression model using tidymodels

Use check_model() to diagnose model assumptions on training data
Diagnose prediction performance using visual diagnostics on test data
Explain overfitting and fit a ridge regression model to address it
Compare two models critically using both metrics and visualisations

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
19.2 Setup — Getting Started
Clone the GitHub Classroom repository for this workshop using the link on your course page. Once cloned, open the RStudio project. You should see the following structure already in place:
bat_workshop/
|
├── .github/
│   └── copilot-instructions.md     # partially complete - review 
├── data/
│   └── bat_methylation.csv          # raw — never modify this file
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_explore.R
│   └── 02_model.R
├── outputs/
└── README.md
Raw data lives in data/ and should never be modified. Scripts are numbered to indicate the order they should be run. All plots and saved outputs go to outputs/. Open scripts/01_explore.R and confirm the packages load without errors.

library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)
library(GGally)
library(corrplot)







NoteTroubleshooting



If any package fails to load, install it with install.packages("package_name") and try again. Run install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "janitor", "skimr", "GGally", "tidymodels", "glmnet", "performance", "corrr")) to install everything needed for both scripts at once.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
Part 1 — Data Exploration
Before building any model it is essential to understand the data you are working with. This section asks you to produce a structured exploration of the bat methylation dataset covering completeness, distributions, and relationships between variables.
You have two options for how you complete this section. Read both before deciding.


Option A — Write the code yourself
Option B — Use GitHub Copilot Chat in agent mode



Open scripts/01_explore.R and write the exploration code yourself using tidyverse. This is the recommended approach. The questions below will guide what you need to produce, and a full solution is available once you have made a genuine attempt.


If you choose to use AI assistance, you must do so correctly or the output will not be trustworthy. Read the guidance below before generating anything.

19.2.1 If you are using GitHub Copilot Chat
Navigate to your repository on github.com. Open Copilot Chat and choose between agent mode or ask mode. Agent mode reads your repository structure and generates code specific to your project. Ask mode does not.






ImportantAgent Mode vs Ask Mode




Ask mode answers questions. You could have one window open on your codespace and one on Github chat window
Agent mode will implement file changes directly on your code on Github. You will need to determine what to keep and what to discard with pull requests. Changes made on Github will then need to be pulled onto your local workspace in order to move forward.



Before you type anything into Copilot, check and improve the copilot-instructions.md file
This file informs all of your requests in Agent or Ask mode on Github Copilot
Copilot Instructions

[COMPLETE: 

Your instructions must be specific enough that the output is verifiable. They should cover the file path and format of the dataset, what the rows and columns represent biologically — Copilot has no biological knowledge unless you provide it — each specific check you want performed, each plot you want produced and where to save it, and the coding conventions to follow: |> not %>%, here() for file paths, snake_case for naming, and a plain English comment at the start of every block explaining what it does and why.










WarningA script that runs is not a script that is correct



Running without errors means the syntax is valid. It does not mean the analysis is correct. You must verify every output against what you know about the data. Work through the questions below before accepting anything the AI produced.




19.2.2 What to produce
Whether you wrote the code yourself or used AI assistance, you should have produced the following outputs before answering the questions:

A report of dataset dimensions — number of rows and columns
A column-level report of missing values
A check for duplicate rows with an explicit count
A histogram of bat ages saved to outputs/

Faceted histograms of methylation values at each CpG site saved to outputs/

A pairs plot showing relationships between age and CpG sites saved to outputs/

A cleaned dataset saved to data/bat_methylation_clean.csv



19.2.3 Step 1 — Loading and cleaning
The data file is a CSV located at data/bat_methylation.csv. Load it using here() for a robust file path and clean the column names immediately after loading.

If you are unsure how here() works or why we use clean_names(), revisit the Data Cleaning chapter before continuing.



Hint: loading and cleaning

The here package builds file paths relative to your project root, which means your script will work on any machine regardless of where the project folder sits. The janitor package provides clean_names(), which converts column names to consistent snake_case — removing spaces, special characters, and capital letters that cause problems in R column references.

library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)

bats_raw <- read_csv(here("data", "bat_methylation.csv"))
bats_raw <- clean_names(bats_raw)

After running this, check that glimpse(bats_raw) shows column names in snake_case with no spaces or backtick escaping needed. For example, CpG 1 TET2 should become cpg_1_tet2.


19.2.4 Step 2 — Checking completeness
Before any analysis, confirm the data are complete. Check dimensions, missing values, and duplicate rows.

The Data Cleaning chapter covers missing value checks and duplicate detection in detail. The skimr package introduced there is particularly useful here because it combines dimension reporting, missing value counts, and distributional summaries in a single call.



Hint: completeness checks

skim() from the skimr package produces a compact summary that includes the number of missing values per column, the completion rate, and basic distributional information for numeric columns. This is more informative than summary() for a first look at a new dataset.
For duplicates, duplicated() returns a logical vector — TRUE for any row that is an exact copy of a previous row. Summing that vector gives the total count of duplicate rows. Reporting zero explicitly is better than producing no output, because silence is not confirmation.

library(skimr)

bats_raw |> skim()

bats_raw |>
  duplicated() |>
  sum()



19.2.5 Step 3 — Distributions
Produce a histogram of bat ages and faceted histograms of methylation values at each CpG site.

Revisit the Data Insights chapter if you are unsure how to use pivot_longer() to reshape multiple columns into a single faceted plot.



Hint: age histogram

A histogram with 15 bins is appropriate for 62 observations. Fewer bins will obscure the shape; more will produce noise. Save directly to outputs/ using ggsave() immediately after the plot call — if you wait until the end of the script you may save the wrong plot.

bats_raw |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of bat ages",
    x     = "Age (years)",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "age_distribution.png"), width = 7, height = 5)




Hint: faceted CpG histograms

With multiple CpG sites measured on different scales, a faceted plot is more informative than overlaid histograms. pivot_longer() reshapes the data from wide format — one column per site — to long format — one row per measurement — which facet_wrap() can then use to split by site.
Use scales = "free_y" to allow each panel its own y-axis scale, since sites with rare extreme values would compress the scale for all other panels if a shared axis were used.

bats_raw |>
  select(-sample, -age_category) |>
  pivot_longer(
    -age,
    names_to  = "site",
    values_to = "methylation"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = methylation)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~site, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Methylation distributions by CpG site",
    x     = "Methylation value",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "cpg_distributions.png"), width = 10, height = 6)



19.2.6 Step 4 — Relationships between variables
Produce a pairs plot showing the relationship between age and all CpG sites simultaneously.

The Data Insights chapter introduces GGally::ggpairs() for visualising relationships across multiple variables at once. Revisit that section if you have not used it before.



Hint: pairs plot

ggpairs() from the GGally package produces a matrix of plots: scatter plots in the lower triangle, correlation coefficients in the upper triangle, and density plots on the diagonal. Select only the relevant columns before passing to ggpairs() to avoid including sample and age_category.

library(GGally)

bats_raw |>
  select(age, starts_with("cpg"), starts_with("aspa")) |>
  ggpairs()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "pairs_plot.png"), width = 10, height = 9)



19.2.7 Step 5 — Save the cleaned dataset
At the end of the exploration script, save a cleaned version of the data that the modelling script can load directly. This avoids repeating cleaning steps and ensures both scripts work from identical data.

# Save the cleaned dataset for use in scripts/02_model.R
# This means 02_model.R does not need to repeat the cleaning steps
write_csv(bats_raw, here("data", "bat_methylation_clean.csv"))


19.2.8 Check your outputs
Work through these questions using your actual outputs. Do not answer from memory or expectation.
Q1. How many bats are in the dataset? 
Q2. How many CpG predictor columns are there, excluding sample, age, and age category? 
Q3. Looking at your age histogram, which best describes the distribution?

Evenly distributed across all agesCertain age groups are over-represented or under-representedConcentrated entirely in adult batsCannot be determined from a histogram

Q4. Looking at your pairs plot, what do you observe about the relationship between CpG sites and age?

Some sites correlate positively with age, some weakly or not at allAll sites correlate equally strongly with ageNo sites show any correlation with ageCorrelations between sites are more important than correlations with age


19.2.8.1 Checkpoint: Before moving to Part 2
Confirm the following before proceeding to the modelling sections.
The dataset has 62 rows and 10 columns. 
TRUE
FALSE
Missing values have been checked at column level using skim() or equivalent. 
TRUE
FALSE
Duplicate rows have been checked and the count reported explicitly. 
TRUE
FALSE
All plots have been saved to outputs/ using here(). 
TRUE
FALSE
Column names are in snake_case following clean_names(). 
TRUE
FALSE
A cleaned dataset has been written to data/bat_methylation_clean.csv. 
TRUE
FALSE



Show complete solution


# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(skimr)
library(GGally)

# Load the bat methylation dataset
# here() builds the path relative to the project root
bats_raw <- read_csv(here("data", "bat_methylation.csv"))

# Clean column names to snake_case
# Removes spaces and special characters that cause problems in R
bats_raw <- clean_names(bats_raw)

# Check dimensions and variable types
glimpse(bats_raw)

# Summarise completeness and distributions at column level
# skim() reports missing values, completion rates, and distributional statistics
bats_raw |> skim()

# Check for duplicate rows — report zero explicitly, not silence
bats_raw |>
  duplicated() |>
  sum()

# Distribution of bat ages
# Uneven coverage of age groups affects model reliability
bats_raw |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of bat ages",
    x     = "Age (years)",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "age_distribution.png"), width = 7, height = 5)

# Methylation distributions at each CpG site
bats_raw |>
  select(-sample, -age_category) |>
  pivot_longer(
    -age,
    names_to  = "site",
    values_to = "methylation"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = methylation)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~site, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    title = "Methylation distributions by CpG site",
    x     = "Methylation value",
    y     = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "cpg_distributions.png"), width = 10, height = 6)

# Pairs plot: relationships between age and all CpG sites
bats_raw |>
  select(age, starts_with("cpg"), starts_with("aspa")) |>
  ggpairs()

#ggsave(here("outputs", "pairs_plot.png"), width = 10, height = 9)

# Save cleaned dataset for modelling script
write_csv(bats_raw, here("data", "bat_methylation_clean.csv"))




What should I look for in the outputs?

Age distribution With only 62 bats, gaps in age coverage matter. A model evaluated on age groups that are sparse in the training data will produce unreliable predictions for those groups. Note which age categories are well represented and which are not — return to this when interpreting the predicted versus actual plot later.
CpG distributions Are methylation values on a comparable scale across sites? Sites that show little variation across bats carry little predictive information. Note any sites with unusually narrow ranges, clustering near zero, or bimodal patterns.
Pairs plot Which CpG sites correlate most strongly with age? Are any sites highly correlated with each other? Strong inter-predictor correlations motivate the use of ridge regression later in the workshop. When you reach the coefficient comparison plot, returning to this pairs plot will help you understand why ridge shrinks certain predictors more than others.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
Part 2 — Machine Learning
From this point forward, you write all code yourself. The sections that follow cover concepts you are encountering for the first time in a machine learning context. Writing the code is part of learning what it does.
Open scripts/02_model.R. All remaining code in this workshop goes here.

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(glmnet)
library(performance)
library(corrplot)
library(here)

# Load the cleaned dataset produced by 01_explore.R
# Run that script first if this file does not exist
bats <- read_csv(here("data", "bat_methylation_clean.csv")) |>
  select(-sample, -age_category)

The select(-sample, -age_category) step removes two columns that must not enter the model:


sample is a text identifier with no biological predictive value — including it would cause an error

age_category is derived directly from age — including it would allow the model to predict age from a column that encodes age, which is circular and would produce falsely excellent results. This is called data leakage.


19.2.9 A familiar starting point
You have used linear regression before to ask whether methylation is associated with age. The code below fits the same model you would have written in your statistics course.

# Fit a linear model the familiar way
# This is the inferential framing you have used before in statistics
lm_base <- lm(age ~ ., data = bats)
summary(lm_base)


Call:
lm(formula = age ~ ., data = bats)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-4.6416 -1.4799 -0.1981  0.9211  5.1378 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
(Intercept)   1.36941    3.56434   0.384  0.70243   
cp_g_1_tet2  -0.21811    0.12888  -1.692  0.09668 . 
cp_g_2_tet2   0.35115    0.12248   2.867  0.00601 **
cp_g_3_tet2  -0.07675    0.08429  -0.910  0.36684   
cp_g_4_tet2   0.10272    0.07517   1.367  0.17775   
cp_g_gria2_1  0.68176    0.22502   3.030  0.00384 **
cp_g_gria2_2  0.33983    0.21835   1.556  0.12581   
aspa_1       -0.08150    0.03785  -2.153  0.03604 * 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.246 on 51 degrees of freedom
  (3 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.645, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5963 
F-statistic: 13.24 on 7 and 51 DF,  p-value: 1.393e-09


summary() gives you coefficients, standard errors, p-values, and R-squared. These answer the question: is there a significant relationship between methylation and age, and how much variance does the model explain?
Look at the output before continuing.
What does the R-squared from summary(lm_base) tell you?

It tells us how accurately the model predicts age in new batsIt tells us how much variance in age the model explains in the data it was fitted onIt is equivalent to RMSE expressed as a proportionIt measures whether the model will generalise to unseen data

What does a p-value on a coefficient tell you in this context?

Whether a predictor is statistically associated with age in this sampleWhether a predictor will improve prediction accuracy on new dataWhether the model is overfittingWhether the predictor should be included in a machine learning model

Now check whether the model meets the assumptions of linear regression before interpreting anything from it.

library(performance)

# Check model assumptions on the training data
# check_model() evaluates the model on the data it was fitted on —
# it tells us whether the model's assumptions are met,
# not whether it will generalise to new bats
check_model(lm_base)

check_model() produces six diagnostic panels. The most important for a linear regression are:


Linearity — does the relationship between predictors and outcome appear linear, or is there a systematic curve in the residuals?

Homogeneity of variance — are residuals equally spread across fitted values, or do they fan out at certain ages?

Normality of residuals — are residuals approximately normally distributed?

Influential observations — with only 62 bats, a single unusual individual can meaningfully shift the fitted line

Note anything concerning before proceeding. These patterns will be worth comparing with the predicted versus actual plot later.






NoteFrom inference to prediction



summary(lm_base) evaluates the model on the same data used to fit it. R-squared and p-values describe how well the model explains the data it has already seen. This is appropriate for inference — but it tells us nothing about how well the model would predict the age of a bat it has never encountered.
For prediction we need a different approach. We withhold some data before fitting, evaluate on that held-out portion, and report performance on data the model never saw. This is what the rest of this section introduces.
The model is identical. The question — and therefore the evaluation — changes entirely.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
The Train/Test Split
If we train a model on all available data and measure how well it fits, we are measuring memorisation rather than generalisation. A model that has seen every data point during training can fit them closely without learning anything that transfers to new observations.

tibble(
  x     = c(0, 0.8),
  xend  = c(0.8, 1),
  label = c("Training set (80%)\nn ≈ 50 bats", "Test set (20%)\nn ≈ 12 bats"),
  fill  = c("#457B9D", "#E63946")
) |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_rect(
    aes(xmin = x, xmax = xend, ymin = 0, ymax = 1, fill = fill),
    alpha = 0.85, colour = "white", linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(x = (x + xend) / 2, y = 0.5, label = label),
    colour = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 4.5, lineheight = 1.3
  ) +
  scale_fill_identity() +
  annotate("text", x = 0.4, y = -0.25,
           label = "Model is fitted on this data only",
           colour = "#457B9D", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.9, y = -0.25,
           label = "Model is never seen this data until evaluation",
           colour = "#E63946", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0.4, xend = 0.4, y = -0.15, yend = 0,
           colour = "#457B9D", arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"))) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0.9, xend = 0.9, y = -0.15, yend = 0,
           colour = "#E63946", arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"))) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.4, 1.2)) +
  labs(
    title    = "The train/test split",
    subtitle = "Data is divided before any modelling begins"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, colour = "grey40")
  )







By withholding some data before training, the training set is used to fit the model. The test set is then held out entirely and used only for final evaluation. The model never sees the test set during fitting, making the evaluation an honest estimate of performance on genuinely new data.
We will run the same model three times, changing one thing at a time. This is the simplest way to understand what drives variability in RMSE — changing two things simultaneously would make it impossible to identify the cause of any difference.


Split 1 is the baseline: 80% training, 20% test, seed 42

Split 2 changes the proportion only: 70/30, seed 42 — everything else identical to Split 1

Split 3 changes the seed only: 80/20, seed 123 — everything else identical to Split 1

Split 1 versus Split 2 tells you whether the proportion matters. Split 1 versus Split 3 tells you whether the random assignment of bats — with the same proportions — affects RMSE.






NoteWhy tidymodels rather than lm()?



lm() fits a model but has no built-in machinery for train/test splits, cross-validation, or consistent evaluation metrics across model types. tidymodels provides all of these in a consistent framework. The underlying fitting engine is still lm() — you can confirm this with set_engine("lm") in the code below. RMSE (root mean squared error) is the average prediction error in the same units as the outcome — here, years.



# Split 1: baseline — 80/20, seed 42
set.seed(42)
split_1 <- initial_split(bats, prop = 0.8)
train_1 <- training(split_1)
test_1  <- testing(split_1)

rec_1 <- recipe(age ~ ., data = train_1) |>
  step_normalize(all_predictors())

fit_1 <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_1) |>
  add_model(linear_reg() |> set_engine("lm") |> set_mode("regression")) |>
  fit(data = train_1)

rmse_1 <- fit_1 |>
  predict(test_1) |>
  bind_cols(test_1 |> select(age)) |>
  metrics(truth = age, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rmse") |>
  pull(.estimate)

rmse_1


# Split 2: 70/30, seed 42
# Only the prop argument changes relative to Split 1
set.seed(42)
split_2 <- initial_split(bats, prop = 0.7)
train_2 <- training(split_2)
test_2  <- testing(split_2)

rec_2 <- recipe(age ~ ., data = train_2) |>
  step_normalize(all_predictors())

fit_2 <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_2) |>
  add_model(linear_reg() |> set_engine("lm") |> set_mode("regression")) |>
  fit(data = train_2)

rmse_2 <- fit_2 |>
  predict(test_2) |>
  bind_cols(test_2 |> select(age)) |>
  metrics(truth = age, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rmse") |>
  pull(.estimate)

rmse_2


# Split 3: 80/20, seed 123
# Only the seed changes relative to Split 1
# The proportion is identical — only the random assignment differs
set.seed(123)
split_3 <- initial_split(bats, prop = 0.8)
train_3 <- training(split_3)
test_3  <- testing(split_3)

rec_3 <- recipe(age ~ ., data = train_3) |>
  step_normalize(all_predictors())

fit_3 <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(rec_3) |>
  add_model(linear_reg() |> set_engine("lm") |> set_mode("regression")) |>
  fit(data = train_3)

rmse_3 <- fit_3 |>
  predict(test_3) |>
  bind_cols(test_3 |> select(age)) |>
  metrics(truth = age, estimate = .pred) |>
  filter(.metric == "rmse") |>
  pull(.estimate)

rmse_3


# Collect results in a readable table
tibble(
  split = c("80/20, seed 42", "70/30, seed 42", "80/20, seed 123"),
  rmse  = c(rmse_1, rmse_2, rmse_3)
)

Record your three RMSE values before answering the questions below.
Should RMSE differ between Split 1 and Split 3 despite identical proportions?

No — RMSE should be identical because the model and proportion are the sameYes — different seeds assign different bats to the test set, producing different evaluation resultsOnly if the dataset is very largeOnly if the model is overfitting

If RMSE changes substantially between Split 1 and Split 3, what does this suggest?

The model is fitted incorrectlyThe proportion should be increased to 90/10A single split may not give a reliable estimate — with small datasets, which individuals land in the test set mattersThe seed should always be set to 42







NoteReflection




Does RMSE change between Split 1 and Split 2? In which direction and why?
Does RMSE change between Split 1 and Split 3, where only the random assignment differs?
If Split 1 and Split 3 give substantially different RMSE values, what does this tell you about how much to trust any single evaluation?
What would you need to do to obtain a more reliable estimate of true predictive performance?

Compare the R-squared from summary(lm_base) — fitted on all 62 bats — with the RMSE from Split 1, fitted on approximately 50 bats and evaluated on 12. The R-squared will almost certainly be higher than the test-set performance suggests is warranted. Why? Which number would you report in a paper?


The sensitivity analysis shows how RMSE varies with split choices. For the main model we now commit to the 80/20 split with seed 42 — the baseline — and proceed with the full tidymodels workflow.

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
Building the Linear Regression Model
A tidymodels workflow has three components. A recipe specifies how to prepare the data. A model specification defines which algorithm to use. A workflow combines the two into a single object that can be fitted, evaluated, and compared.

19.2.10 Why normalise predictors?
For ordinary linear regression, normalisation is not strictly necessary. OLS finds the same fitted values and produces the same RMSE regardless of whether predictors are on their original scale or rescaled. If all your predictors are already on comparable scales — as CpG methylation values broadly are here — you could skip this step for the linear model without consequence.
We include it for one specific reason: ridge regression requires it. The ridge penalty shrinks coefficients towards zero, and that shrinkage is applied to all coefficients equally. A predictor measured in thousands will have a naturally smaller coefficient than one measured in units, and the penalty will shrink the larger-scale predictor more aggressively — not because it is less informative, but because of scale. Normalising first ensures the penalty is applied fairly.
By normalising now and keeping the same recipe for both models, we ensure the comparison between linear and ridge regression is fair.






NoteWhen does normalisation matter?



Normalisation is required when the algorithm is sensitive to predictor scale — ridge regression, lasso, elastic net, k-nearest neighbours, and neural networks all fall into this category.
Normalisation is not required for ordinary linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, or random forests — these are scale-invariant by construction.
A useful rule of thumb: if your model involves a penalty term or a distance calculation, normalise. If it does not, normalisation is a stylistic choice rather than an analytical requirement.



# Commit to the 80/20 split with seed 42 for the main model
set.seed(42)
bat_split <- initial_split(bats, prop = 0.8)
bat_train <- training(bat_split)
bat_test  <- testing(bat_split)

# Recipe: define the outcome, predictors, and preprocessing
# step_naomit() removes rows with missing values before fitting
# glmnet (used by ridge) cannot handle missing values and will error without this
# step_normalize() rescales all predictors to mean 0, standard deviation 1
bat_recipe <- recipe(age ~ ., data = bat_train) |>
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes()) |>
  step_normalize(all_predictors())

# Model specification: ordinary linear regression using the lm engine
lm_spec <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# Workflow: bind recipe and model into a single object
lm_workflow <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(bat_recipe) |>
  add_model(lm_spec)

# Fit on training data only — the test set is not touched here
lm_fit <- lm_workflow |>
  fit(data = bat_train)

Now run check_model() on the fitted workflow to examine assumptions on the training data.

# Extract the underlying lm object from the tidymodels workflow
# check_model() needs a standard R model object, not a workflow
# This runs on the training data only
lm_fit |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  check_model()







NoteTwo diagnostics, two questions



You have now run check_model() twice — once on lm_base fitted on all 62 bats, and once on the tidymodels fit trained on approximately 50 bats.
Both run on training data only. They tell you whether the model’s assumptions are met on the data it has seen. This is a necessary check but not sufficient for a predictive model.
The predicted versus actual plot in the next section asks a different question: how well does the model perform on the bats it has never seen? A model can pass all assumption checks on the training data and still fail to generalise. Both checks are informative and neither replaces the other.


Now generate predictions on the held-out test set and calculate evaluation metrics.

# Generate predictions on the held-out test set
lm_preds <- lm_fit |>
  predict(bat_test) |>
  bind_cols(bat_test |> select(age))

# Calculate evaluation metrics
lm_metrics <- lm_preds |>
  metrics(truth = age, estimate = .pred)

lm_metrics




.metric
.estimator
.estimate



rmse
standard
1.9092471


rsq
standard
0.7103651


mae
standard
1.5756454






What does RMSE measure?

The average squared error in yearsThe average prediction error in the same units as the outcome — here, yearsThe proportion of variance in age the model explainsThe maximum error across all predictions

If RMSE is considerably larger than MAE, what does this suggest about the error distribution?

RMSE and MAE are always equalA small number of bats are being predicted very poorly — most errors are small but a few are largeThe model is underfittingAll errors are consistently moderate across the age range







NoteReflection



The output contains three metrics.


RMSE is in years. Is your value acceptable given the biological application? If you were determining whether a wild bat is reproductively mature, how large an error would matter?

RSQ is R-squared on the test set: the proportion of variance in age explained by the model on held-out data. How does this compare with the R-squared from summary(lm_base) on the full dataset?

MAE is mean absolute error. If RMSE is considerably larger than MAE, what does this tell you about how errors are distributed across the age range?

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
Visualising Model Fit
RMSE is a useful single-number summary but it conceals how errors are distributed across the age range. A model can produce a reasonable RMSE while systematically failing in ways that matter biologically. The predicted versus actual plot and the residual plot are the most important diagnostics available alongside check_model().
The dashed red line represents perfect prediction. The blue smooth curve shows where predictions actually sit relative to that ideal. Any systematic deviation from the diagonal is a finding, not a cosmetic issue.


Hint: what am I looking for?

Two patterns are worth looking for specifically in this dataset. First, does the blue curve deviate from the diagonal at extreme ages — particularly for older bats, which are underrepresented in the training data? Second, does the residual plot fan outward at higher predicted ages, suggesting the model is less reliable for older bats than younger ones? Both patterns would have been visible in the pairs plot from Part 1 if you looked carefully at the scatter around the regression lines at the high end of the age range.


# Predicted versus actual age
# The dashed red line is perfect prediction
# The blue smooth curve shows where predictions actually sit
lm_preds |>
  ggplot(aes(x = age, y = .pred)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "blue", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    x     = "Actual age (years)",
    y     = "Predicted age (years)",
    title = "Linear regression — predicted vs. actual age"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()








# Residual plot: actual minus predicted age, against predicted age
# A well-behaved model has residuals scattered randomly around zero
lm_preds |>
  mutate(residual = age - .pred) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = .pred, y = residual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", colour = "blue", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    x     = "Predicted age (years)",
    y     = "Residual (actual minus predicted)",
    title = "Residual plot — linear regression"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()







What is the primary reason for examining the predicted versus actual plot alongside RMSE?

The model fits training data well and will generalise to new batsThe model may be systematically biased at certain ages — RMSE alone would not reveal thisThe model assumptions are violated and it should be abandonedThe residual plot and predicted versus actual plot always tell the same story

Which residual pattern would suggest the model is less reliable for older bats than younger ones?

A fanning pattern — residuals becoming more spread at higher predicted agesResiduals scattered randomly around zero with no patternAll residuals falling exactly on the zero lineA curved pattern that dips below zero at low predicted ages







NoteComparing check_model() with the predicted vs. actual plot



You ran check_model() on the training data earlier. Compare those diagnostics with what you see in the predicted versus actual and residual plots now.

Do the same patterns appear in both sets of diagnostics, or does the test set reveal problems that the training data did not?

check_model() evaluates assumption violations on data the model has seen. The predicted versus actual plot evaluates generalisation on data the model has not seen. Which is more relevant for the biological application — deploying this model to age wild-caught bats?
A model can pass all assumption checks on training data and still fail to generalise. What would that look like in these two sets of plots simultaneously?

title: 19  Machine Learning Regression
text:
Why Ridge Regression?
Linear regression with many predictors faces a specific problem. When predictors are numerous relative to observations — as is typical in genomic data — the model can assign large coefficients to CpG sites that correlate with age in the training data by chance rather than because of a genuine biological relationship. On new data, those coefficients produce poor predictions. This is overfitting: the model has learned the training data too specifically to generalise.

19.2.11 Returning to the correlation structure
Before introducing ridge regression, return to the correlation structure you observed in Part 1. We now examine it on the training data only — the test set must remain untouched, even for exploratory purposes.

# Correlation structure in the training data only
# We never use bat_test for exploratory purposes
library(corrplot)

bats_cor_matrix <- bats |> 
  drop_na() |> 
  select(-age) |> 
  cor()



corrplot(bats_cor_matrix)













NoteWhat the correlation structure tells us



Two problems emerge from correlated predictors in linear regression.
Multicollinearity: if two CpG sites carry similar information about age, linear regression cannot reliably apportion credit between them. Small changes in the data produce large swings in their individual coefficients even though their combined contribution is stable. This makes individual coefficient estimates untrustworthy even when the overall predictions are reasonable.
Spurious correlations: a model with many correlated predictors has more opportunity to find correlations in the training data that do not hold in new data. Some of what looks like signal is noise that happens to correlate with age in this particular sample of 62 bats.
Ridge regression addresses both problems by shrinking coefficients towards zero. Correlated predictors are constrained together rather than one being arbitrarily inflated at the expense of the other.


What is the primary consequence of multicollinearity for linear regression coefficients?

It makes the model impossible to fitIt inflates RMSE on the training dataIt makes individual coefficient estimates unstable even when combined predictions are reasonableIt always causes overfitting regardless of sample size


19.2.12 Fitting ridge regression
Ridge regression adds a penalty to the fitting process that discourages large coefficients. All predictors are retained, but their influence is constrained. The strength of this constraint is controlled by lambda (λ):

Lambda = 0: no penalty — identical to ordinary linear regression
Lambda very large: all coefficients shrunk close to zero — the model predicts close to the mean age for every bat
The right value sits between these extremes and must be chosen empirically

We choose lambda using cross-validation. The training data is divided into five equal portions called folds. The model is trained on four folds and evaluated on the fifth, repeated five times so every fold serves as the evaluation set once. Performance is averaged across all five repetitions, giving a stable estimate at each lambda value without using the test set.










Hint: why cross-validation and not the test set?

If we used the test set to choose lambda, we would be making a modelling decision based on the test set. That decision would be optimised for this particular held-out sample rather than for genuinely new data. The test set would no longer be a fair evaluation of performance on unseen observations — it would have influenced the model indirectly. This is a form of data leakage.


# Ridge specification
# mixture = 0 specifies ridge; mixture = 1 would give lasso
# penalty = tune() tells tidymodels to search across candidate lambda values
ridge_spec <- linear_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 0) |>
  set_engine("glmnet") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# Use the same recipe as before so both models are directly comparable
ridge_workflow <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(bat_recipe) |>
  add_model(ridge_spec)

# Create 5-fold cross-validation folds from the training data only
set.seed(42)
bat_folds <- vfold_cv(bat_train, v = 5)

# Define 50 candidate lambda values to evaluate
lambda_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(), levels = 50)

# Fit at every lambda value across all five folds
ridge_tuned <- ridge_workflow |>
  tune_grid(
    resamples = bat_folds,
    grid      = lambda_grid,
    metrics   = metric_set(yardstick::rmse)
  )


# Plot cross-validation RMSE against lambda
# Each point is the average RMSE across all five folds at that lambda value
p <- autoplot(ridge_tuned)
  
p + scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_log()) # force better x axis labels









Hint: reading the tuning curve

The x-axis shows lambda on a log scale — values increase from left to right. The y-axis shows average RMSE across the five folds at each lambda value.
At the far left, lambda is close to zero and the model behaves like ordinary linear regression. If RMSE is high here, overfitting is present — the model fits the training folds well but generalises poorly to the validation fold.
Moving right, increasing lambda forces coefficients towards zero. If there is a clear minimum in the curve, that is the sweet spot where the bias introduced by the penalty is outweighed by the reduction in variance.
At the far right, lambda is so large that all coefficients shrunk close to zero and the model predicts close to the mean age for every bat — RMSE rises again because the model has lost all predictive information.







NoteReflection: reading the tuning curve



Before running the selection code, interpret the plot.

At very low lambda values on the left, is RMSE high or low? What does this tell you about whether overfitting is present?
As lambda increases moving right, what happens to RMSE? Is there a clear minimum?
What happens at very high lambda values on the far right, and why?
Write down where you would place lambda based on visual inspection alone. Do this before running the next block.



Now select the best lambda:

# Approach: the single lambda with the lowest cross-validation RMSE
best_lambda <- ridge_tuned |>
  select_best(metric = "rmse")

best_lambda
